text: It is based on toys which transform from cars to robots.
correct: based on toys which transform from cars to robots. [Try to maintain unity when it comes to contractions. Either use them or don't. Although it isn't incorrect to break unity -- and indeed, doing so can be a useful stylistic technique when done correctly -- it usually sounds strange or unprofessional if you don't know what you're doing. Of course, for casual writing, it doesn't really matter. As for the second correction I've made in this sentence, the reason you'd be better off saying "into" here instead of just "to" is because when you say the phrase "from cars to robots," it sounds kind of like you're describing the scope of the transformers' transformation abilities rather than the starting and ending point of their transformations. That is, it sounds like you're saying that "they can transform into anything, from cars to robots [i.e., including cars, robots, and everything inbetween]," rather than "they can start out as cars and then transform into robots" -- but of course you mean the latter, not the former. If you use "into," this becomes the only possible interpretation, but if you use "to," the sentence could potentially be misinterpreted, which is why "to" doesn't sound quite as good as "into" in this context.]

text: Anyone of them who grabs his partner to his side of the line will get a point.
correct: Any who could grab their partner to their side of the line got a point. The last sentence was past tense. There are two choices you could make to stay consistent in this story: explain the rules, by using a consistent present tense while talking about this particular game. "Anyone who grabs their... gets a point..." Or by telling a story of what actually happened and using consistent past tense. "Anyone who grabbed/ could grab their partner...".

text: So he didn't wait for his turn to go there; he just did it simultaneously, so they both got to double their points and win equally.
correct: This is because they didn't wait for their respective turns to go there; they just did it simultaneously, so they both doubled their points and won equally. When we say "simultaneously" it must be two people and not one person. I found it easier here to describe what they were both doing at the same time. Hence, I put "respective turns." Which means their turns in the order given. And that it applies to both.

text: Now, I understand what people mean by: "We are in a tough world right now".
correct: Now, I understand what people mean when they say that "we live in a tough world right now." This is an example of a quote that fits neatly into the sentence. So no comma or colon is necessary, but the second quotation mark goes after the period. You could use "mean by" if you are just talking about a word or concept. Like if I say I know what people mean by "tough." Without the colon, too. But if you are talking about a quote or a popular saying it is hard to work it into the sentence this way. A colon is useful with a quotation if you say something like... And here is what he said: "..." or something like that.

And so the reader develops a vision in his mind that no other person could make (similar to a fingerprint). "makes his own touch" is not a metaphor that would be used. Someone has their own touch, generally. "Touch" is also not used with thinking. Same with designing. Though I can understand what you mean... so I put "develops" and shorted this a bit. I also like the fingerprint part in parentheses because it is safer than using a comma. As is making this long sentence into two.

text: After all that, it might seem like I don't like watching movies but actually, I could watch dozens of them whenever it's possible.

I changed the first part to a more common grammatically order. "It might not" is more common when talking about a negative than "I might seem that I don't". I also used the synonym "enjoy" to avoid saying "like" twice. "but actually I" is less common than "but I could actually". And of course the comma goes before "but" as that word is most common conjunction that goes with a comma.

text: If he say black is white,It will be so for us...
correct: If he were to say black is white, It would be so for us... [What to say here depends on whether you consider your father's authority a good thing or a bad thing. You might say something like: "For us, if he says black is white, then it is so." However, to me at least (and probably to others as well), that evokes something similar said about Hitler: "If [Hitler] says 2+2 = 5, then it is so." In other words, what that sentence means is that he (the subject of the sentence) has too much authority for anyone's good, that the people under him are oppressed, might as well not exist, etc. etc. On the other hand, if you want to emphasize his authority, the respect he gets, etc., and consider it a good thing, you would say: "To us, whatever he says, is so." or "To us, what he says, goes.", both of which get rid of the negative connotations in things like "black is white" or "2+2=5".]

text: My city is called Guadalajara, the second largest city in Mexico.
correct: My city is called Guadalajara, and it is the second largest city in Mexico. (OR: My city is Guadalajara, the second largest city in Mexico.) (There is a subtle difference that I would like to explain: When you say something is "called" something in English, the expectation is that you are defining it--that is it unknown to the reader. So, if you define your city, the reader won't know that it's the second largest city, so you need another main clause (or at least a "which;" see below). On the other hand, if you just state what the city is, you can further describe it with a subordinate clause. Here's another example: "I'm called 'Sprachjunge,' which is a German compound nickname." I could also say "I'm called 'Sprachjunge,' and this name is German." Contrast with "I'm Sprachjunge, a German compound name.")

text: And I hope everyone could have nice new year's holidays!

There are certainly a few exceptions here, but in general that's how it works.

[By "he", do you mean your son? You say that you received the invitation, but "he" (which has to be someone else) can't go. "He" can't refer to Mike, because it is Mike's birthday party, so Mike presumably sent the invitation. "Him" at the end of the sentence is also confusing, since you have been using the pronoun "he," but "him" presumably refers to Mike, not to whoever "he" is in the sentence.]

text: (C-1) Which is it hotter, in Okinawa or in Tokyo?

text: (b) This is the same watch as I bought in London.
correct: (b) This is the same watch as I bought in London.[This sentence is grammatically incorrect. It should be written instead: "This is the same of watch I bought in London." You really cannot say: "This is the same watch as the one I bought in London," although people would understand that you really mean it's the same . The connotation is that there is more than one watch: "," which you bought in London, and a replica, which you are comparing with "the one." The word "as" indicates that a comparison is being made.]

text: I think you use both phrases "be at the playground" and "be in the playground".
correct: I think you use both phrases "be at the playground" and "be in the playground". [Alternatives: I think both of the phrases "..." and "..." are used. I think both of the phrases... are possible.]

correct: My guess is that you like "in" better than "at" in this context. [Alternative: My guess is that "in" is better in this context. Note: I don't know why, but "I suppose (that)" sounds slightly belligerent, as if you are saying that it is unreasonable for the reader to have this preference.]

text: Some people try to read carefully and slowly when they find some sentences difficult to understand, but it is completely wrong.
correct: Some people try to read carefully and slowly when they find some sentences difficult to understand, but this is a mistake. [Alternative: "... when they come across a sentence which is difficult to understand, ..."]

text: (B) I haven't found my umbrella, which I lost a week ago.
correct: (B) I haven't found my umbrella, which I lost a week ago. [In this sentence, which is correct as it stands, "which I lost a week ago" is an appositive clause, not a restrictive clause. "My umbrella" already specifies which umbrella is being talked about, and "which I lost a week ago" gives further information about it-- information which is not necessary to the meaning of the sentence. It does not restrict the subject "my umbrella" any further.]

text: (B) Don't you think you could do well on this exam?
correct: (B) (1) Did you think you couldn't do well on this exam? (2) Did you think you would do this badly on this exam? (3) Did you think you would do well on this exam? (4) Didn't you think you could do well on this exam? [All of these are possible, but have different meanings. (1) and (4) ask the student if he or she gave up without even trying before or during the test because he or she didn't think there was a possibility of doing well. (2) and (3) ask the student if he or she realized during the test and afterwards that his or her work was so poor. minicat-san's variant does not sound natural to me, but that may be a difference between UK English and US English.]

text: I mean, they think such TV commercials were not criticized before in (A), but already received some criticism in (B).
correct: I mean, they think such TV commercials were not criticized before in (A), but (have) already received some criticism in (B). [This is an awkward use of "they." "They" should only be used to refer to more than one person, but it seems like you are saying that one person who says (A) thinks one thing, and one person who says (B) thinks something else. As you stated it, "they" both think both things. Alternative: "I mean, a person who says (A) thinks such TV commercials have not been criticized in the past, but a person who says (B) assumes that they have already been criticized to some extent."]

text: In the morning we met a couple, supposedly in their 60s, from Perth in Australia.

(but we could clearly hear their French accent)" Also, you can indicate the area/country a location is in by simply adding it after a comma. text: Technically, when something important happened to me, i must feel something special in my mind.
correct: Technically, when something important happens to me, I feel something special. The phrases I crossed out are unnecessary. The word "must" is kind of implied here, since it's describing an emotional reaction to something. The phrase "in my mind" is also not needed, because in English the concept of feeling with one's head or mind doesn't really exist. We tend to "think" with our heads and "feel" with our hearts. However, the phrase "in my mind" can be used to mean "in my opinion," and if you meant it in this way I would correct the sentence like this: Technically, in my mind, when something important happens to me, I should feel something special. Basically, the first version is just a statement of how you react to experiencing something important, and the second version is stating how you (and presumably others) should react when important things happen to you (them).

text: But I think the most important reason is that the boots made me look taller about 4~5cm.
correct: But I think the most important reason is that the boots make me look about 4~5cm taller. By using "make," the reader knows every time you wear the boots, you look taller. They didn't make you look taller just the first time you tried them on. At least, I hope not. ^_^; Also, putting "taller" at the end of the sentence is a little more natural, but if you kept taller where you originally had it, you'd need to say, "make me look taller by about 4~5cm." Also, in English, the ~ isn't used for approximations. Instead, English uses the n-dash (-), so it'd be "4-5cm." In your diary, it isn't a big deal to use the ~, but if you're writing papers for your English classes in the UK, you'll probably need to use the -.

text: I have been worried about the homestay in London these days, since I called a friend who is living in London now and asked her about that.

It's more natural to say "it" here. The only time I can think of that you would use "that" instead of "it" is when you're referring to something somebody else said. text: Do you want your lover to unburden its troubles to you if he/she is suffering from something painful?
correct: Do you want your lover to unburden their troubles to you if he/she is suffering from something painful? [Okay, so this is the tricky bit. "their" is a plural word, and it is improper to use it in the singular sense as I am here. it is used incredibly frequently by just about everyone but English majors writing thesis papers to mean what I'm using it for here: a non-gender-specific form of "his" or "her." If you ever do write an English thesis paper, the proper way would be to put "his/her," as in "Do you want your lover to unburden his/her troubles to you if he/she is suffering from something painful?" You can't use "its" because that is reserved for objects, so it sounds really weird when you're using it for people. But the way that just about anyone would say this while speaking normally would be "Do you want your lover to unburden their troubles to you if they're suffering from something painful?"]

text: The expense is $30 per an hour and the reason why I selected it was that it is cheaper than other private tuturings.

(Your phrase sounds very impersonal in English, because you talk about a face-to-face lesson with another person without mentioning her at all. I understand that you want to create a distance between yourself and a teacher that you did not appreciate. I have used a fairly impersonal term ("her rate") to talk about the cost, but I changed "selected" to "chose" to make it sound more human. You choose people (for example: a husband), but you select things (for example: a wedding ring or a wedding date). The word "cheaper" can mean both "less expensive" and (by implication) poorer quality. By using "she asks less" instead, I have changed the implication to "she is not greedy", which sounds more tactful.)

text: I got a day off last Friday for having a great time with my husband.
correct: I got day off last Friday a great time with my husband. (1) You could say "I got a day of recently". In this case, you would use "a" because you do not tell us which day it was. Using "a" tells us "don't bother to imagine the precise date: you don't need to know it". However, you say "last Friday". In this case, you should use "the" to warn us: "you should already know, or you are about to learn, details about which day it was". (2) "for having" indicates a deliberate cause and effect. It could mean "Because of my success in having a great time with my husband, my company awarded me with a day off" or "My company specifically wanted me to have a great time with my husband, and arranged for me to have a day off for that purpose". My suggestion of "and had" only indicates that the two events are connected.

"could" indicates something that you did often in the past (for example: "I found I could get to school quicker if I took my bicycle"), or something that you have not yet decided to do in the future. text: Halfway through the lesson, she made a minor mistake, which, trying to be a good tutor, I corrected her.
correct: Halfway through the lesson, she made a minor mistake, which, trying to be a good tutor, I corrected . (her is not used here)These sentences which used "which" in the middle of the sentence in between commas are a little confusing. I'll explain:Halfway through the lesson she made a minor mistake which, trying to be a good tutor, I corrected.Lets try reading the sentence with just the blue parts:Halfway through the lesson she made a minor mistake which I corrected.This sentence is easy enough to understand, right? and grammatically correct.In the middle of the sentence this has been added "..., trying to be a good tutor,..." This has been added into the sentence to give extra information. Let's try this sentence again.This has been added into the sentence, enclosed within commas, to give extra information. Do you see how extra information is added? As for "which":In this sentence you could say "Halfway through the lesson she made a minor mistake which I corrected" or "Halfway through the lesson she made a minor mistake and I corrected her". Don't worry, this isnt a mistake that you've made. The example is just wrong. The problem with this example is that it has many commas (this: ,,,,) which confuses you. And in addition the grammar is wrong in the first place. You may say "...which I corrected" or "and I corrected her" but you can't mix the two and say "which I corrected her".Which in this example is part of what has been said just before it. It means that you are correcting the mistake, not the girl. Therefore a "her" at th end is not needed. When you say "and I corrected her". The "and" is adding extra information, it is not part of the previous sentence. Therefore the grammar required that you state the object/subject of the sentence again. But like I said before the example is wrong to beginw ith. So don't worry. ^_^
